description: Quiz testing comprehension of William Tell Chapter 10 from Roots and Wings Class 6 Literature Reader

questions.question: What was William Tell's main weapon that he carried with him?
questions.explanation: William Tell carried a crossbow (bow and arrows) as his main weapon, which was most closely associated with him throughout the story.

questions.question: Who placed the hat on the pole in the market square?
questions.explanation: Gessler, the cruel Austrian governor, placed his hat on a pole in the market square and ordered all people to bow before it as a symbol of the Emperor's authority.

questions.question: What was William Tell's position in society?
options: A skilled archer and proud Swiss patriot
questions.correctAnswer: A skilled archer and proud Swiss patriot
questions.explanation: William Tell is described as a skilled archer and a proud Swiss patriot who refused to submit to Austrian rule, making him a symbol of freedom and resistance.

questions.question: Why did William Tell secretly take out a second arrow?
options: To impress Gessler with his skill
As a backup in case he missed the apple
To kill Gessler if his first shot harmed his son Walter
To show his strength to the other people
questions.correctAnswer: To kill Gessler if his first shot harmed his son Walter
questions.explanation: William Tell kept a second arrow hidden with the intention that if his first shot accidentally hurt or killed his son, the second arrow was meant for Gessler as revenge.

questions.question: William Tell was an excellent archer.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly describes William Tell as a skilled archer, and he successfully shot the apple off his son's head, demonstrating his exceptional archery abilities.

questions.question: William Tell bowed before Gessler's hat because he forgot about the order.
questions.explanation: Although William Tell initially walked through without bowing because he was talking to his son and not paying attention, when soldiers forced him to bow, he refused on principle as a proud Swiss patriot who would not submit to foreign rule.
